Article.ArticleTitle: Cross-education after forearm fracture: a randomised controlled trial.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study aimed to examine whether isokinetic exercises applied to the intact wrist could reduce muscle strength loss and prevent the development of Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS) type I in adults with distal radius fractures.

Forty-two patients with distal radius fractures were randomized into two groups: a Cross-Education (CE) group (n = 21) and a control group (n = 21). Both groups received a standard home exercise program. In addition, the CE group performed 12 supervised sessions of isokinetic wrist strengthening exercises on the non-fractured side during the immobilization period. After cast removal, isometric muscle strength of the fractured wrist was evaluated, and patients were monitored for the development of CRPS type I.

The CE group showed significantly higher isometric muscle strength in wrist flexors and extensors compared with the control group (wrist flexors: d = 0.73, p = 0.024; wrist extensors: d = 0.87, p = 0.008). Furthermore, the incidence of CRPS type I was significantly lower in the CE group (p = 0.03).
Cross-Education training applied during immobilization can prevent muscle weakness and decrease the risk of CRPS in fracture rehabilitation.
These findings highlight CE as a safe and effective early intervention strategy that may enhance functional outcomes and reduce complications in patients with distal radius fractures.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics Approval: This is a prospective, randomised, double-blind interventional study, for which ethical approval was obtained from the Istanbul Medipol University Non-Interventional Clinical Research Ethics Committee: Decision No.: 266, Date: 14/03/2024. The study was registered with the ClinicalTrials (Registration Number: NCT06562218). All experimental procedures were performed in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants were blinded to group assignment, and outcome evaluations were performed by an assessor who was blinded to the intervention. Conflict of interests: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest. The results of the study are presented clearly, honestly, and without fabrication, falsification, or inappropriate data manipulation. The authors did not receive support from any organization for the submitted work. All authors certify that they have no affiliations with or involvement in any organization or entity with any financial interest or non-financial interest in the subject matter or materials discussed in this manuscript.
